title: Sensor Setup: Building the DShield Honeypot and SIEM
body: I'm running this on a Raspberry Pi 5 with SANS Internet Storm Center's DShield sensor software — everything lives on my home network rather than in the cloud. The Pi handles two honeypot services: Cowrie, which fakes an SSH/Telnet server, and isc_agent, a web honeypot that mimics a vulnerable HTTP/HTTPS site. I also stood up the DShield-SIEM project (the community Elastic Stack built specifically for DShield sensors — Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana, Filebeat, all running in Docker) so I'd have my own copy of the data to dig through, on top of whatever gets reported back to SANS.

Internally, Cowrie sits on 2222 (SSH) and 2223 (Telnet), and the web honeypot listens on 8000 and 8443. The DShield installer moved my actual SSH access to port 12222 automatically, which kept it from clashing with Cowrie. On the outside, ports 22, 23, 80, and 443 get NAT'd down to those internal ports, so anyone scanning the usual suspects lands in the honeypot instead of anywhere near real management access.

My ISP is AT&T, and I've got their gateway in IP Passthrough mode so the public IP passes straight through to my UniFi Dream Wall, which does the actual routing and firewalling. The Pi sits in its own DMZ zone, kept separate from everything else on my network, using UniFi's zone-based firewall setup.

Getting traffic to actually reach the honeypot took two separate steps for every port, since UniFi treats NAT forwarding and firewall permissions as two different things: port forwards on WAN2 (my real AT&T-facing interface) mapping 80→08000, 443→8443, 22→2222, and 23→2223 to the Pi's DMZ address, plus matching firewall Allow rules (External → DMZ, with return traffic auto-allowed) for each port. Without the Allow rules, nothing got through no matter what the NAT rules said — canyouseeme.org wouldn't show anything open until both pieces existed.

Activity ends up in two places. The sensor reports back to SANS ISC on its own, feeding into the larger threat intel pool from sensors like mine all over the world. Separately, Filebeat ships the raw logs — Cowrie sessions, web honeypot hits, iptables entries — over to the DShield-SIEM stack, which I'm running on a KVM VM on my own PC. That's what gives me the pre-built DShield Kibana dashboards for looking at where attacks come from, what gets hit, login attempts, and whatever commands get typed during a session.

The honeypot didn't become reachable just from setting up port forwards — I had to add the firewall Allow rules separately before anything got through. Even after that fix, traffic still wasn't landing on the sensor; it turned out the port forward was tied to WAN1 instead of WAN2, which is the interface AT&T actually uses. Switching it over fixed that.

The bigger headache was getting the Pi and the DShield-SIEM VM talking to each other. The Pi's DMZ zone and the VM's KVM/libvirt network had zero path between them — both worked fine on their own, but Filebeat couldn't reach Logstash at all. I ended up adding a firewalld port-forward on my PC (routing port 5044 over to the VM) plus a UniFi rule letting the Pi reach my PC on that port. Then I hit a second layer of the same problem: libvirt has its own separate reject-everything rule that doesn't care about general port forwarding settings, so 5044 had to be allowed there too before traffic would actually get through.

Once the network path was sorted, Logstash still wouldn't open its listening port; the pipeline status sat at "red." Digging through the logs showed the Elasticsearch output was failing because it referenced an ILM policy (cowrie.ttylog) that didn't exist yet. Creating the missing policies got the pipeline running.

Last issue was just running out of disk space once real traffic started pouring in. I mounted an SMB share off my UniFi UNAS Pro 4 into the DShield-SIEM VM, set it up as a snapshot repository, and scheduled nightly backups of the cowrie indices. After that I updated the ILM policies to delete local copies 14 days after rollover, so the disk stays manageable while everything's still archived on the NAS first.
